description_long: This object is a roughly leaf-shaped stone with a pointed tip and a broad, slightly convex base, resembling a prehistoric tool or projectile point. Its surface is uneven and marked by visible flake scars, suggesting it was shaped by deliberate chipping or knapping. The coloration is predominantly off-white to light beige, with some brownish stains and small patches of discoloration, possibly from soil or mineral deposits. One side features a handwritten marking, likely an identification number, indicating it may be part of a collection or archaeological find. The stone is relatively thin, as seen from the side views, and tapers to a sharp edge along its perimeter. Its overall appearance is weathered but intact, with no major cracks or breaks.
description: A leaf-shaped, chipped stone with a pointed tip, off-white color, and visible flake scars, resembling an ancient tool or projectile point. The surface is weathered with some brown stains and a handwritten marking.
description_view_0: The front view shows the pointed tip, broad base, and irregular flake scars, with brown stains visible on the surface.
description_view_1: The side view reveals the stone's thin profile and tapered edges, highlighting its sharpness and chipped contours.
description_view_2: The back view displays a similar shape to the front but features a handwritten marking and fewer visible stains.
description_view_3: The opposite side view again shows the thinness and edge sharpness, with minor surface discoloration.
attribute_location: archaeological site or urban park display
attribute_placement: set as a standalone feature or displayed on a flat surface
attribute_pattern: Irregular flake scars and chipping marks, with some mottled brown stains and a handwritten number on one side.
attribute_weathering: Surface is rough and weathered, with minor discoloration and no significant cracks, indicating moderate exposure to natural elements.
attribute_integration_with_surroundings: Contrasts with smooth, modern surfaces but could blend with natural stone displays or archaeological exhibits; not integrated with vegetation.
